ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori prawns; closest parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tandoori-marinated prawns; 'Crustaceans (cooked)' (A00LC) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955003 'Prawn (substance)' is the closest available term; no specific tandoori preparation code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tandoori prawns consists of approximately 6 medium-to-large tiger prawns (around 250 g total), served with mint chutney and sliced onions.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Prawns, cooked (tandoori-style preparation estimated from grilled prawn and marinade components)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for cooked prawns. Tandoori preparation involves marination in yogurt and spices followed by high-heat tandoor cooking. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data with adjustments for yogurt marinade contribution (adds modest fat and protein) and moisture loss from tandoor cooking (~20% moisture reduction). Sodium is elevated relative to plain cooked prawns due to salt and spice marinade. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) for validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Seafood and protein-dominant foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori prawns. GI is estimated at 5 based on the established principle that high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood and grilled meat preparations have very low GI values (typically 0–10) as confirmed by the University of Sydney GI Database for analogous protein-dominant foods. Carbohydrate content per 100 g is approximately 3.2 g, predominantly from yogurt and spice marinade. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (5 × 8.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.4, classified as negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade and spices (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1300 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant serving of tandoori prawns can contribute approximately 1300 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g per 100 g) and high protein (~22.5 g per 100 g) with negligible glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori prawns are a suitable protein-rich, low-glycemic option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as the very low carbohydrate content and high-quality protein support glycaemic control without causing significant postprandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content (~155 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium; however, low saturated fat and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids are beneficial
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While prawns contain moderate dietary cholesterol and the marinade contributes sodium, the low saturated fat content and presence of omega-3 fatty acids make tandoori prawns a conditionally acceptable choice for cardiovascular disease patients when consumed in moderate portions and prepared with minimal added oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Prawns (तंदूरी झींगा, Tandoori Jhinga) is a classic North Indian seafood dish in which large tiger prawns are marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture — typically comprising red chilli, cumin, coriander, turmeric, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice — and then cooked at high temperature in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The tandoor imparts a characteristic smoky char and slightly crisp exterior while keeping the prawn flesh moist and succulent inside. The dish is typically served with fresh mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, tandoori prawns are a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 22.5 g protein and only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making them an excellent choice for low-glycemic and high-protein dietary patterns. The fat content is modest at approximately 4.8 g per 100 g, with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio. Sodium is elevated due to the spice marinade (~520 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-suitable. In Singapore, tandoori prawns are served at North Indian restaurants and are popular as a starter or sharing dish. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ~5), and the glycemic load per restaurant serving is negligible, making this dish appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is rich in selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine from the prawn base, supporting thyroid function and neurological health.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori prawns?
Are tandoori prawns good for diabetics?
Is tandoori prawns high in protein?
How much sodium in tandoori prawns?
What is the GI of tandoori prawns?
Can I eat tandoori prawns if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are used in tandoori prawns?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for tandoori prawns exists as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data with defensible adjustments for yogurt marinade and tandoor moisture loss, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database principles for high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood preparations. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) with adjustments for yogurt-spice marinade contribution and approximately 20% moisture loss during tandoor cooking. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180 (cooked shrimp). No exact prepared-dish entry exists in any approved primary source for tandoori prawns as served in restaurants. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for cooked prawns; USDA data for cooked shrimp reports negligible vitamin D and this value is not reliably established for the tandoori preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in prawn-based preparations with plant-derived marinade oils — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked shrimp. 4. GI is estimated at 5 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood foods from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for tandoori prawns was found in peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (5 × 8.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.4. 5. Sodium value (~520 mg per 100 g) is an estimate reflecting the spiced marinade; actual sodium will vary by restaurant preparation. 6. Halal status is assumed based on prawn and plant-based ingredients; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code for tandoori prawns exists in any of the three systems as of the accessed date. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA sources for cooked prawns and are approximate for the tandoori preparation.
